aboutText: Burrows in sand in surf areas.

Pelagic in neritic and oceanic waters, sometimes near the bottom (Ref. 5217, 58302). Small juveniles may be found near the shore (Ref. 5217); adults near bottom to depths of 60 m (Ref. 26938). Feed on sedentary or slow moving crustaceans and occasionally feed on small crabs and fishes (Ref. 9283). Juveniles are attractive aquarium fish, but do not do well in captivity (Ref. 12484). Excellent food fish (Ref. 9626); marketed fresh or dried or salted (Ref. 9283).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Alectis ciliaris 106710346.jpg" by Alberto Alcalá (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Uncommon species (Ref. 34024).
Found inshore, along beaches and down to at least 16 m over a muddy bottom. Enters estuaries, but perhaps not tolerating very low salinities (Ref. 12225).
imageAttribution: "Ilisha argentata Meek & Hildebrand" by Seth E. Meek and Samuel F. Hildebrand is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs or rocky bottoms (Ref. 5217). Found among gorgonians. Occurs in pairs (Ref. 5521). Feeds mainly on sponges, gorgonians, and algae (Ref. 9710) but also eats hydroids and stinging coral (Ref. 13442).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Cantherhines macrocerus 242638710.jpg" by Dan Schofield (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits rocky or coral reefs (Ref. 9710). Juveniles in channels and on inshore reefs (Ref. 9710). Feeds primarily on sponges (Ref. 9710). Small juveniles do well in aquariums once they begin to accept food.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Holacanthus bermudensis.jpg" by NURC/UNCW and NOAA/FGBNMS (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs (Ref. 5521). Drifts with bended tail to prey on small fish. Retreats into holes or empty worm tubes. Ignores divers (Ref. 9710). Often seen with schools of masked gobies (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Arrow blenny (Lucayablennius zingaro) by q phia - Cuba 2017.jpg" by q phia (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Adults are found over soft bottoms of the continental shelf; sometimes forming schools near the surface (Ref. 5217). Marine pelagic species very common in coastal lagoons and estuaries (Ref. 57392). They feed on fish, cephalopods, zooplankton and detritus (Ref. 28587). Juveniles common in brackish estuaries and often associated with jellyfish (Ref. 5217). Marketed fresh and salted.
imageAttribution: "Chloroscombrus chrysurus.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Adults occur usually over mud and sandy mud bottoms in coastal waters and in estuaries where the nursery and feeding grounds are located. They feed mainly on worms, crustaceans and fishes. An excellent foodfish. Sold fresh and frozen; eaten fried, broiled, microwaved and baked (Ref. 9988).
imageAttribution: "Micropogonias undulatus.jpg" by United States Fish Commission (Public domain).

aboutText: Generally found in surface waters near the coast (Ref. 5217). Capable of leaping out of the water and gliding for long distances above the surface (Ref. 3613).
imageAttribution: "Cheilopogon pinnatibarbatus from Aquas.JPG" by SR EXR (Public domain).

aboutText: A solitary species (Ref. 26340) occurring in shallow, inshore areas. Found on rock, coral, or mud bottoms (Ref. 5217). Juveniles found in mangrove areas and brackish estuaries (Ref. 5217). Large adults may be found in estuaries (Ref. 5217). Adults appear to occupy limited home ranges with little inter-reef movement. Feeds primarily on crustaceans, particularly spiny lobsters as well as turtles and fishes, including stingrays. Territorial near its refuge cave or wreck where it may show a threat display with open mouth and quivering body. Larger individuals have been known to stalk and attacks divers. Over-fished, primarily by spear fishing (Ref. 9710). Marketed fresh and salted. Meat is of excellent quality. Important game fish (Ref. 9342). Reported to reach weights of more than 315 kg (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Epinephelus itajara - Museu da Ciência da Universidade de Coimbra - University of Coimbra - Coimbra, Portugal - DSC09236.jpg" by Daderot (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Inhabits mud or sand bottoms (Ref. 5217). Most common on rather shallow offshore banks, such as commercial shrimp grounds; less common inshore.
imageAttribution: "Porichthys plectrodon photophores.jpg" by US NOAA (Public domain).

aboutText: Adults usually found near the bottom from inshore waters to at least 54 m depth, but may form schools near the surface (Ref. 5217). Juveniles are found on muddy bottoms brackish estuaries and in coastal marine waters (Ref. 5217). Adults feed on small fishes and crustaceans. Good food fish and marketed fresh.
imageAttribution: "Selene setapinnis.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs inshore, over muddy bottoms and in brackish or hyper-saline littoral lagoons (Ref. 5217). Forms schools.

Abundant in shallow coastal waters, from mangroves and sandy beaches to wrecks and harbors. Juveniles (black phase) are common in estuaries and often found in very shallow water swimming at an angle resembling dead leaves or as infertile red mangrove pods and other debris. Adults often occur in very large schools of up to 500 individuals (Ref. 9710). Feed on benthic invertebrates like crustaceans, mollusks, annelids, cnidarians as well as on plankton (Ref. 35237). Good food fish (Ref. 5521); marketed fresh (Ref. 5217). Often circles divers (Ref. 9710). Minimum depth from Ref. 9710. In southeastern Brazil found between 23 and 45 m (Ref. 47377). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35425).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Atlantic spadefish (Chaetodipterus faber).jpg" by Symbiosis (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Migrates in large schools over great distances along the shore. Larvae are found in surface waters between 19.6° and 29.8°C with salinities of 28.3 to 37.4 ppt. Feeds mainly on small fishes (clupeoids and anchovies), few quantities of penaeoid shrimps and cephalopods. Casting, live-bait fishing, jigging, and drift fishing are also employed in capturing this species. Aerial spotting is sometimes used in locating the fish. Marketed fresh, frozen or smoked; eaten pan-fried, broiled and baked.
imageAttribution: "Scomberomorus maculatus, Atlantic Spanish Mackerel.jpg" by J. Deeds (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabit harbors and shallow coastal areas (Ref. 9710). Pelagic (Ref. 27549). Form schools (but solitary individuals reported), probably not entering water of low salinity. Feed by filtering plankton (copepods), but also take small fishes, crabs and shrimps. Marketed fresh, frozen and salted; also used in the fishmeal industry. Occur in freshwater in St. Johns River, Florida (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Opisthonema oglinum.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs on sand or mud bottoms and on beaches, especially on the surf (Ref. 57343).
imageAttribution: "Atlantic threadfin ( Polydactylus octonemus ).jpg" by NOAA's Fisheries Collection / NOAA Photo Library (Public domain).

aboutText: An inshore, surface-dwelling fish which forms sizeable schools (Ref. 3723). Feeds on small fishes and plankton (Ref. 3723). Mainly used as bait for offshore game fishes such as sailfishes and marlins; utilized as a food fish in the West Indies (Ref. 3723). Minimum depth range from Ref. 84211.
imageAttribution: "Hemiramphus balao FMIB 45852 Hemiramphus guineensis.jpeg" by Pieter Bleeker (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits deeper reefs.

An inshore, surface-dwelling species forming sizeable schools (Ref. 3723). Feeds mainly on sea grasses and small fishes (Ref. 3723). Mainly used as bait for offshore game fishes such as sailfishes and marlins; utilized as a food fish in the West Indies (Ref. 3723).
imageAttribution: "Hemiramphus brasiliensis 63969255.jpg" by Louis Imbeau (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: A common species ranging from coastal bays to 75 m depth (Ref. 34024). Inhabit soft bottoms (Ref. 5217). Oviparous, with oval pelagic eggs floating in a gelatinous mass (Ref. 205). Flesh is appreciated (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in protected shallow waters, in coral, rock or sand bottoms (Ref. 5521) especially in seagrass beds (Ref. 7251). Carnivorous; diet may vary according to seasonal and spatial changes in prey species abundance. Preys on a few species of amphipods as juveniles and shift to diet of crabs, shrimp and isopods (Ref. 94105).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Inhabit coral reefs. Occur singly or in pairs. Feed on polychaete worms, coral polyps, crustaceans and mollusk eggs. Oviparous (Ref. 205), monogamous (Ref. 52884). Form pairs during breeding (Ref. 205). Adults may form plankton-feeding aggregations of up to 20 individuals, and occasionally clean other reef fishes which join the group, such as grunts, parrotfishes and surgeon fishes. (Ref: 40093). Generally common (Ref. 9710).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Chaetodon striatus 327997138.jpg" by Jay Brasher (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Found over muddy bottoms, often near estuarine areas (Ref. 9626). Feeds mainly on worms (Ref. 3702). Marketed mostly fresh and salted (Ref. 3702).
imageAttribution: "Paralonchurus brasiliensis.jpg" by Albert C.L.G. Günther (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits sandy bottoms near coral reefs (Ref. 9710). Feeds on gastropods, crabs and fishes (Ref. 13628). Monogamous (Ref. 52884). Oviparous, mouthbrooding by males (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Opistognathus macrognathus 207184187.jpg" by Pauline Walsh Jacobson (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Occurs in pockets of coral rubble (low coral growth on white sand bottom), usually in the vicinity of sea fans (Ref. 5521, 7251, 9710). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Micrognathus crinitus - pone.0010676.g032.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Abundant in all inshore habitats where there is adequate cover, such as seagrass beds and reef flats. Feeds on mollusks, crustaceans and echinoderms (Ref. 36453). Generally common (Ref. 9710). Traded as an aquarium fish at Ceará, Brazil (Ref. 49392).
imageAttribution: "Bandtail puffer (Sphoeroides spengleri).jpg" by Andrew David, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC Panama City; Lance Horn, UNCW/NURC - Phantom II ROV operator. (Public domain).

aboutText: Probably preferring moderately deep water in reef areas (Ref. 35505). Particulate planktivore feeding in the water column (Ref. 35505).
imageAttribution: "Parasphyraenops atrimanus" is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Prefers hard bottoms at depths of about 55 m.
imageAttribution: "Centropristis ocyurus.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Found in intertidal areas in Bermuda. Occurs in deeper water from 30-110 m in USA.
imageAttribution: "FMIB 52189 Emblemaria atlantica Jordan Pensacola, Fla.jpeg" by David Starr Jordan (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits shallow water on soft calcareous bottoms. Found usually near burrow, but occasionally seen swimming in small schools.
imageAttribution:
"Archivos do Museu Nacional do Rio de Janeiro (IA archivosdomuseu21muse).pdf" by
Museu Nacional (Brazil)
(Public domain).

aboutText: Common in clear insular areas or in coral reefs off mainland coasts (Ref. 5217). Juveniles frequent areas with algae (e.g. Sargassum) (Ref. 26235). Usually in schools which may be spawning groups; occasionally solitary. Feeds on fishes, shrimps and other invertebrates. Marketed fresh (Ref. 56217). Easily approached (Ref. 9710). Large individuals have caused ciguatera when eaten (Ref. 13442).
imageAttribution: "Bar jack Caranx ruber (2412820133).jpg" by Paul Asman and Jill Lenoble (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits coastal waters, usually over sandy mud bottoms (Ref. 3702). Feeds mainly on shrimps (Ref. 3702). Important food fish.
imageAttribution:
"Estada em Pernambuco, em 1666, de François de Lopis, marquês de Mondevergue, segundo o relato de Souchu de Rennefort (1688) - habitantes, costumes, escravidão (...).pdf" by
Nelson Papavero, Argus Vasconcelos de Almeida, Dante Martins Teixeira
(CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Commonly found in bays, harbors, and on the continental shelf. Inhabits soft bottoms. Traded as an aquarium fish at Ceará, Brazil (Ref. 49392).
imageAttribution: "Scorpaena brasiliensis 159886663.jpg" by Pauline Walsh Jacobson (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Found on sandy and muddy bottoms of coastal waters, estuaries and mangroves, and occasionally in the surf (Ref. 57343). Young aggregate near river mouths (Ref. 57343). Common around islands (Ref. 9710). Nocturnal feeder, taking crustaceans, chaetognaths, plant material, and polychaetes (Ref. 57343). May have a prolonged spawning season (Ref. 57343).
imageAttribution: "Polydactylus virginicus.jpg" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits shallow sandy and rocky areas of coral reefs (Ref. 9710). Feed on copepods, amphipods, delicate algae and occasionally of eggs of Abudefduf (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution:
"Bulletin - United States National Museum (IA bulletinunitedst4731898unit).pdf" by
United States National Museum
Smithsonian Institution
United States. Dept. of the Interior
(Public domain).

aboutText: Ubiquitous, from gravel pits and rock ledges at the sea's edge to clear-water reefs. Remains hidden in cracks and crevices during the day (Ref. 9626). Sometimes observed between the spines of Diadema antillarum (Ref. 9626).
imageAttribution:
"Journal of the Straits Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society. (IA mobot31753002848262).pdf" by
Royal Asiatic Society of Great Britain and Ireland.
(Public domain).

aboutText: Found along sandy shores and over shallow muddy bottoms (Ref. 3798). Adults feed at night on crustaceans and small fishes (Ref. 3798). Marketed fresh (Ref. 3798).
imageAttribution: "Conodon nobilis.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: A solitary species (Ref. 26340) inhabiting rocky and coral reefs (Ref. 9710). Always found near cover (Ref. 9710).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Hypoplectrus puella, Puerto Rico.jpg" by NOAA (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits patch reefs (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Coryphopterus thrix - pone.0010676.g168.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Found near the water's edge on rubble bottoms (Ref. 7251). Also found in coral reefs with clear water (Ref. 13628).
imageAttribution: "Barbulifer ceuthoecus" is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Adults occur in seagrass beds, coral or rocky reefs and sandy areas. Also found around mangrove shores and sponge beds; less common on flourishing coral reefs (Ref. 26938). They remain within 50 cm from the substrate. Adults feed on algae, polychaetes, amphipods, foraminiferans and gastropods while juveniles feed on harpacticoid copepods, nemerteans and polychaetes (Ref. 9626). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205). Caught incidentally in traps and small-meshed beach nets (Ref. 5217).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Stegastes leucostictus (beaugregory) (San Salvador Island, Bahamas) 1 (16145503926).jpg" by James St. John (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits coral and rocky areas, often near drop-offs. Solitary or in groups in caves or holes (Ref. 9710). Often seen hovering in the spines of long-spined urchin.
imageAttribution: "Apogon townsendi - pone.0010676.g072.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Occurs from water's edge to at least 18 m depth, often in silty water. Synchronously hermaphroditic. Not a coral-reef species (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Serranus subligarius.jpg" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: A schooling species occurring in coastal waters. Apparently common (at least in August, but not in July). Specimens with well developed eggs were taken in June, 1927, suggesting a summer spawning period.

Inhabits shallow waters, over turtle grass, sand or rocky bottom and around coral reefs (Ref. 3725). Young commonly found among floating Sargassum seaweeds (Ref. 3725). Feeds on plants, mainly on benthic algae, as well as on small crabs and mollusks (Ref. 6547, 44187). Also feeds on spinner dolphins’ feces and vomits at Fernando de Noronha Archipelago, southeast Atlantic. The offal feeding may be regarded as a simple behavioral shift from plankton feeding to drifting offal picking (Ref. 48727). Marketed fresh (Ref. 3725).
imageAttribution: "Bermuda or yellow chub Kyphosus sectatrix incisor (2414878136).jpg" by Paul Asman and Jill Lenoble (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Adults inhabit shallow coral reefs and isolated patch reefs in deeper water. Feed primarily on algae but also on polychaetes, hydroids, copepods and ascidians (Ref. 9626). Aggressively territorial but only around a small area (Ref. 9710). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205). Taken incidentally in traps and small-meshed beach nets (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Stegastes partitus 364317997.jpg" by cello caruso-turiello (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Occurs in coastal waters, forming dense schools. Not entering the mangrove-lined lagoons of the Venezuelan mainland, but is the dominant species of the Los Roques Archipelago off Venezuela and there found as much in the lagoons as outside them. Frequently caught in mixed schools (with A. mitchilli) in the Miami area. Feeds on zooplankton. Ripe females recorded off Florida in June and July. More data needed.
imageAttribution: "Anchoa lamprotaenia" by David S. Jordan is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Generally found over vegetated sand grounds and over mud bottoms in mangrove areas (Ref. 3722). May form aggregations. Probably feeds on small benthic invertebrates (Ref. 5217). Marketed fresh but not highly esteemed; processed into fishmeal (Ref. 3722).
imageAttribution:
"Descriptive Enumeration of a Collection of Fishes from the Western Coast of Central America, Presented to the Smithsonian Institution, by Capt. John M. Dow (IA jstor-4059774).pdf" by
Gill, Theodore
(Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in surf beaches, usually in less than 3 m depth. Burrows in soft sandy bottom, where it waits for prey, with only the eyes, nose and mouth protruding.
imageAttribution: "Dactyloscopus crossotus" by William Beebe is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Found in shallow coastal waters over sandy mud or soft mud bottoms (Ref. 3702). Feeds mainly on small shrimps (Ref. 3702). Important food fish.
imageAttribution: "Isopisthus parvipinnis (7949997652).jpg" by Biodiversity Heritage Library (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Occurs in shallow neritic areas, over shallow mud to sand-mud bottoms; less common in areas with clear water and coral reef habitats (Ref. 5217). Marketed fresh and salted (Ref. 3798).
imageAttribution: "Haemulon chromis - 1829 - Print - Iconographia Zoologica - Special Collections University of Amsterdam - UBA01 IZ13000160.tif" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: A solitary species (Ref. 26340) inhabiting shallow reefs (Ref. 9710). Feeds on fishes and crustaceans (Ref. 26180). Often found near the bottom around soft and hard corals. Occasionally been seen spawning with the H. chlorurus (Ref. 26938)
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Hypoplectrus nigricans - Reef1714.jpg" by NOAA Photo Library (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Inhabits larger patch reefs and quickly sloping rocky bottoms. Often near the shelter of caves, ledges, or wrecks (Ref. 9710). Feeds at night on crustaceans, mollusks, smaller fish, and the long-spined urchin, Diadema. Marketed fresh.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "FMIB 38074 Anisotremus surinamensis (Bloch) Pompom.jpeg" by Barton Warren Evermann / Millard Caleb Marsh (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabit clear seaward reefs. More common around oceanic islands (Ref. 9276). Found on inner and outer reef crests, usually near the slope or drop-off to deeper water, where in small but loose aggregations. Sometimes swim high above substrate feeding on zooplankton (Ref. 48637). May also be solitary (Ref. 90102). Benthopelagic (Ref. 58302). Diet consists primarily of calcareous algae and zooplankton (Ref. 1602); also feed on phytoplankton (Ref. 5213). At Fernando de Noronha Archipelago, southeast Atlantic, groups of about 100 individuals join groups of spinner dolphins to feed on feces and vomits of the cetaceans when they congregate in a shallow bay for rest and social interactions. The postures a dolphin adopts prior to defecating or vomiting are recognized, and the fish begin to converge to the dolphin shortly before the actual voiding. Offal feeding may be regarded as a simple behavioral shift from plankton feeding to drifting offal picking (Ref. 48727). Marketed fresh (Ref. 9770). Minimum depth reported taken from Ref. 128797.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Melichthys niger 1866.jpg" by G. H. Ford (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits rocky shallows (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Starksia fasciata range.png" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Starksia lepicoelia.jpg" by Carole C. Baldwin (Public domain).

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Commonly found in clear seagrass beds; uncommon on reefs or muddy bays (Ref. 9710). A protogynous hermaphrodite (Ref. 55367). Generally of no interest to fisheries because of its small average size (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Halichoeres poeyi - pone.0010676.g120.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Adults inhabit patch reefs surrounded by white sand bottom (Ref. 5521). Eggs are hemispherical and covered with numerous sticky threads that anchor them in the algae on the nesting sites (Ref. 240). Larvae are planktonic which occur primarily in shallow, nearshore waters (Ref. 94114).
imageAttribution: "Biodiversity Assessment of the Fishes of Saba Bank Atoll, Netherlands Antilles.pdf" by Jeffrey T. Williams; Kent E. Carpenter; James L. Van Tassell; Paul Hoetjes; Wes Toller; Peter Etnoyer; Michael Smith, edited by Brian Gratwicke (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Inhabits rocky tide pools, eroded limestone slopes and basins, and algae-covered rocks surrounded by sand (Ref. 5521). Also found in areas with coral (Ref. 13628).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Adults occur in clear Thalassia testudinum beds where it often inhabits empty snail shells (Ref. 9626). Caught at night or in the early hours of the morning (Ref. 9626).
imageAttribution: "Astrapogon puncticulatus - pone.0010676.g073.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Found over mud or mud-sand bottoms of estuaries and adjacent areas (Ref. 5217). Feeds on bottom-dwelling organisms, mainly worms. Marketed fresh and salted.
imageAttribution:
"Die Naturgeschichte des Thierreichs (IA CAT10623976001).pdf" by
Giebel, C. (Christoph), 1820-1881
(Public domain).

aboutText: Observed to mainly perch on live coral surfaces and terminal phase males in holes, also typically within live coral heads. It has been reported to be widely distributed down to 20 m in Cayman Islands (Ref. 125603).

Inhabit small patch reefs surrounded by fine white sand (Ref. 5521). It is usually found on live corals or gorgonians, often photographed nestled in the grooves of brain corals, with the TP males occupying holes in live corals or sponges; depth range extends to 20 m at least (Ref. 125603).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Found on continental and insular shelves, mainly over sandy, shell, and coral bottoms. Feeds on small fishes, including pinfish (Sparidae) and porcupine fish. Preyed on by larger sharks. Viviparous (with a yolk-sac placenta), with 3 to 6 young per litter. Performs a 'hunch' display, with back arched, caudal lowered and head raised, as a possible threat display when confronted by divers. Utilized dried salted for human consumption. Minimum depth from Ref. 055176.
imageAttribution: "Carcharhinus acronotus (FDA 109).jpg" by The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits mixed patches of sand and turtle grass (Ref. 5521). Rare (Ref. 9710). Has been observed buried in the sand with only the head showing (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Quassiremus ascensionis.jpg" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: A stenohaline species associated with rock or coral reef habitat (Ref. 40540).

Adults are common above deep outer reefs and feeds in aggregations of the small zooplankton, primarily copepods (Ref. 13442). Often with creole wrasse. They retreat into coral crevices when frightened (Ref. 9710). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Azurina cyanea 345536272.jpg" by Dan Schofield (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Mostly restricted to clear, sheltered, shore waters with luxuriant plant growth, usually from 3-10 m depth. Adults feed mainly on crustaceans (Ref. 7251).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution:
"The fishes of North and Middle America - a descriptive catalogue of the species of fish-like vertebrates found in the waters of North America, north of the Isthmus of Panama (IA fishesofnorthmid02jord).pdf" by
Jordan, David Starr, 1851-1931
Evermann, Barton Warren, 1853-1932
(Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs. Juveniles found on Thalassia beds (Ref. 13628). Feeds on benthic plants and small organisms in the sand (Ref. 5521). Forms large spawning aggregations (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "FMIB 38168 Scarus Coeruleus (Bloch) Blue Parrot-Fish; Loro.jpeg" by Barton Warren Evermann / Millard Caleb Marsh (Public domain).

aboutText: A schooling species (Ref. 5217, 57391) generally not far from the coast (Ref. 5217), in coastal marine and brackish waters to at least 100 m depth (Ref. 57392). Juveniles often found in association with floating Sargassum (Ref. 5217), often entering lagoons and estuaries (Ref. 57392). Adults feed on fishes, shrimps, and other invertebrates. They spawn offshore from January through August (Ref. 26938). Eggs are pelagic (Ref. 4233). Excellent food fish (Ref. 9626); marketed fresh, frozen (Ref. 5521), and salted. Often used for bait (Ref. 26938). Maximum reported total length 550mm in Ref. 57392.
imageAttribution: "Blue Runner (Caranx crysos).jpg" by El pescador Informatico (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs, inshore grassy or rocky areas. Forms small groups (Ref. 26235). Mainly diurnal. Feeds entirely on algae (Ref. 5521). At Fernando de Noronha Archipelago in southwestern Atlantic, juveniles hold cleaning stations together with the doctorfish (Acanthurus chirurgus) and sergeant major (Abudefduf saxatilis) and graze algae as well as pick molted skin and parasites from green turtles (Chelonia mydas). This behavior is preceded by a characteristic inspection usually followed by feeding nips on the turtles’ skin (head, limbs, and tail), as well as on the carapace. The most inspected and cleaned body parts are the flippers (Ref. 51385). Diurnal species (Ref. 3194). It forms resident spawning aggregations in late afternoon after high tide (Ref. 27825, 120681, 120682). The spine on both sides of the caudal peduncle may inflict painful wounds (Ref. 5217). Marketed fresh. Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 127989.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Acanthurus coeruleus - blue tang - Bay of Pigs - Cuba.jpg" by Tisquesusa (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits reef areas, inshore bays and seagrass beds. Feeds mainly on zooplankton and small benthic animals, but may also feed on ectoparasites of other fishes (Ref. 9626). Spawn at midday throughout the year (Ref. 26938). A protogynous hermaphrodite (Ref. 55367). Generally of no interest to fisheries because of its small average size (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Caribbean Bluehead Wrasse, Thalassoma bifasciatum, North Atlantic Ocean, BQ imported from iNaturalist photo 128594911.jpg" by no rights reserved (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Found in reef areas, with its foraging behaviour suggesting that like the other hamlets, it is a mesopredator. In Bocas del Toro and Guna Yala (Panama), it has been observed at depths ranging from 2 to 12 m.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Hypoplectrus affinis, St John 00830, USVI imported from iNaturalist photo 475446963.jpg" by (c) Josiah Londerée, some rights reserved (CC BY) (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits seagrass beds and weedy areas with sand (Ref. 9710). Feeds on seagrasses (Ref. 9710). Buries in the sand to sleep in a mucus tube (Ref. 9710). Protogynous hermaphrodite (Ref. 55367).
imageAttribution: "Cryptotomus roseus 137456681.jpg" by jeyre (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Found in small groups over coral and rocky reefs and drop-offs (Ref. 9710). Juveniles are abundant in Thalassia beds (Ref. 5217). Feeds on crustaceans, bivalves, and occasionally on small fishes. Marketed fresh.
imageAttribution: "Blue Stripe Grunt. Haemulon sciurus.jpg" by Brian Gratwicke (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits worm tubes in seagrass beds, often in turbid inshore waters. Usually solitary. Very territorial (Ref. 9710). Occasionally found to depths of 12 m (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Chaenopsis ocellata 161286894.jpg" by Pauline Walsh Jacobson (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Adults are coastal species. They do not form large schools (Ref. 35237). Juveniles are encountered in brackish estuaries (Ref. 5217), moving to greater depths as it grows, but not deeper than 50 m (Ref. 9626). Eggs are pelagic, spawning occurs generally in summer (Ref. 35237). Marketed fresh (Ref. 9626).
imageAttribution: "Fish4276 - Flickr - NOAA Photo Library.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC. (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coastal waters (Ref. 7251). Generally found near shore, to depths of 10 m. Both adults and juveniles frequent estuaries and surf zones (Ref. 27549). Bottom feeder with fish, clams, worms and shrimps. It causes a number of injuries to bathers and waders (Ref. 26938). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 50449). Litters of 2-4 pups; gestation 10-11 months, including a period of embryonic diapause (Ref. 114953).
imageAttribution: "Hypanus say Mandalay Bay Shark Reef Las Vegas.jpg" by big-ashb (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits littoral (subtidal) waters; usually found on sandy or rock bottom; large fish may be found in waters less than 1 m of depth hiding in crevices or caves (Ref. 3273). Feeds mostly on molluscs and crustaceans. Reported to be moderately abundant and regularly consumed by fishermen (Ref. 50841). Eggs are deposited on the shells of mollusks and on stones. One of the very few marine fishes that guards fry and juveniles. It is caught in traps and special types of hooks (garrapinos) (Ref. 50841). Marketed fresh.
imageAttribution: "Amphichthys cryptocentrus 98542022.jpg" by A H (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs. A 62 mm SL female specimen (ANSP 115182) contained about 100 embryos (3-4 mm TL) and about 50 eggs (diameter 0.5 mm). The embryos with brown eyes, but no pigmentation on head and body is probably due to the 44 years of preservation (Ref. 57883).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in open water (Ref. 9710); sometimes found near the coast, mainly near oceanic islands (Ref. 5217). Sometimes found over coral heads (Ref. 9710). Feeds on zooplankton and small fishes. Marketed fresh.

Inhabits shallow coastal waters, estuaries and bays, over sand and mud bottoms (Ref. 3237). Often in schools, except large individuals which are solitary (Ref. 7251). Dorsal fin may show above surface (Ref. 42064). More or less pelagic but feeds on benthic worms, crustaceans, and mollusks (Ref. 2850); that are picked from mud and sand bottoms (Ref. 27549). Tolerates oxygen poor water by inhaling air into a lung-like airbladder (Ref. 9710). Flesh is bony and not esteemed (Ref. 9268). One of the most important game fishes worldwide (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Albula vulpes head.jpg" by Ruff tuff cream puff (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Found on the continental and insular shelves, on inshore and coastal areas, over mud and sand bottoms, also on coral reefs. Often occur in shallow water including estuaries, shallow bays and over coral reefs (Ref. 9987). Spends night time hours on shallow grass flats, searching for nocturnally active invertebrate prey, moves into deeper water during the day (Ref. 27549). Feed mainly on crustaceans, also on bivalves, octopi, and small fish. Viviparous, with 6 to 9 young per litter. Size at birth about 35 to 40 cm. Not territorial. Always occurs in small groups. Considerable sexual segregation occurs. Shows diel rhythm of activity. Utilized for human consumption and processed for fishmeal.
imageAttribution: "Sphyrna tiburo (FDA 100).jpg" by The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (Public domain).

aboutText: Found in oceanic waters (Ref. 9626), sometimes over coral heads (Ref. 9710). Swims in schools to depths of 64 m (Ref. 26938). Feeds on zooplankton and small fishes.
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Adults inhabit shallow, coral or rocky bottoms and sometimes near the shore in areas with little visibility (Ref. 9626). Juveniles were collected from tide pools and on rocky Lithothamnion reefs; adults found up to 15 m depth (Ref. 40395). A diurnal species, adults feed on benthic algae, polychaetes and harpacticoid copepods (Ref. 9626); also consume hydroids and sponges and has been observed to feed on Abudefduf saxatilis eggs at night (Ref. 9626). 16:21 SL/TL (Ref. 9626). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding; eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate; males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Stegastes fuscus 344696006.jpg" by Tsssss (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Inhabits coastal waters, on sand or mud bottoms (Ref. 26340). Common along sandy shorelines, sometimes near coral reefs (Ref. 12951). Aggregates in shallow waters ca. 10-20 m deep during summer and autumn months, dispersing to deeper waters during winter (Ref. 114953). Buries itself with only eyes protruding (Ref. 12951). Nocturnal, moves to shallow bays at night to feed; prefers worms, but may take juvenile snake eels, anemones, and, small crustaceans (Ref. 12951). Produces broods of 4 to 15 young (Ref. 26938). Reaches ca. 45 cm TL. Both sexes mature by 27 cm TL; birth size at ca. 11 cm TL (Ref. 114953). Can discharge between 14 and 37 volts. Contact with the skin can produce a severe electric shock. In addition to the main electric organ, this species possesses a bilateral accessory electric organ (Ref. 10011) speculated to have a possible role in social communication (Ref. 10489). Reported to taste good, but not fished commercially. Traded as an aquarium fish at Ceará, Brazil (Ref. 49392).
imageAttribution: "Narcine brasiliensis - 1700-1880 - Print - Iconographia Zoologica - Special Collections University of Amsterdam - UBA01 IZ14200041.tif" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in shallow water, over soft bottoms.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Eugerres brasilianus.jpg" by Carla Isobel Elliff (CC BY 3.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0

aboutText: Found in coastal waters, often forming compact schools. Maximum depth range based on spawners in Brazil (Ref. 114041). Probably similar to S. aurita, but if they are indeed distinct, then this species may account for the double spawning peaks of S. aurita. Constitutes a large fishery in Venezuela, but statistical reports are not separated from S. aurita. Marketed fresh and canned (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Sardinella brasiliensis" by Silas Stearns is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Inhabits shallow coastal waters, on sandy and muddy bottoms, and not normally found in lagoons and estuaries (Ref. 244). Feeds on small bony fishes, including sardines and anchovies, also shrimp and squid (Ref. 244). Viviparous, with 1 to 4 young in a litter; size at birth 33 to 34 cm (Ref. 244). Its flesh is highly appreciated and is marketed dried salted.
imageAttribution: "Rhizoprionodon lalandii SI.jpg" by D Ross Robertson (Public domain).

aboutText: Most common in seagrass beds, but occurs from water's edge to 75 m depth. Commonly trawled on shallow shrimp grounds.
imageAttribution:
"Manuscript of Henry Weed Fowler on the fishes of the Philippines, unpublished, circa 1930-1941 (IA manuscripthenry00fowldp).pdf" by
Fowler, Henry Weed, 1878-1965
(Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs usually on or near coral reefs (Ref. 7251). Rare, most often found from seagrass beds (Ref. 13442).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Chilomycterus antennatus - 1700-1880 - Print - Iconographia Zoologica - Special Collections University of Amsterdam - UBA01 IZ15500141.tif" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits clear white sandy areas near deep reefs and grassy and rocky areas. Burrows in the sand and the male guards the eggs (Ref. 5521). The Bridled goby, which has X-shaped marks and spots, occurs in both clear and more murky inshore waters, while the paler, very similar Sand goby, Coryphopterus tortugae [= Coryphopterus glaucofraenum], occurs over white sand (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Coryphopterus glaucofraenum - Reef1727.jpg" by NOAA Photo Library (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Forms dense schools, often in shallow waters close to shore. Able to tolerate a wide range of salinities, from hypersaline to almost fresh. Feeds on copepods when young, then on gastropods, foraminifers and an occasional ostracods and annelid. Breeding was recorded in April through to July at Beaufort, North Carolina. Breeds in harbors, estuaries and sounds. Eggs are elliptical, transparent, without oil globule, the yolk appearing `cellular' (Ref. 189). Great variation. Principal parasites are nematodes and cestodes (Scolex polymorphus and Rhynchobothrium sp.) and trematodes (Distomum appendiculatum and Distomum sp). Marketed as food (Ref. 37032).
imageAttribution: "Broad-striped anchovy ( Anchoa hepsetus ).jpg" by NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC. (Public domain).

aboutText: A benthic species and solitary species (Ref. 26340) inhabiting outer reef slopes. Occurs under ledges and in holes of reef slopes (Ref. 37816). Has the habit of puffing its head like a cobra when approached (Ref. 37816). Secretive and nocturnal (Ref. 48635). Minimum depth reported taken from Ref. 30874.
imageAttribution: "FMIB 33357 Channomuraena Vittata.jpeg" by David Starr Jordan (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in coral and rocky areas with little sand.
imageAttribution: "Biodiversity Assessment of the Fishes of Saba Bank Atoll, Netherlands Antilles.pdf" by Jeffrey T. Williams; Kent E. Carpenter; James L. Van Tassell; Paul Hoetjes; Wes Toller; Peter Etnoyer; Michael Smith, edited by Brian Gratwicke (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Coral-dwelling goby (Ref. 51183). Found in groups near coral heads (Ref. 9710). Monogamous (Ref. 52884). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35408).
imageAttribution: "Appendix A - benthic survey - USACE-p16021coll7-23123.pdf" by CSA Ocean Sciences, Inc. (Public domain).

aboutText: Found only around islands (Ref. 51183).
imageAttribution: "Starksia smithvanizi range.png" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Generally in protected areas such as bays or inlets; rarely in insular areas with clear oceanic water and coral reefs (Ref. 5217). Juveniles encountered near the shore. Feeds on small crustaceans and mollusks (Ref. 3798). Taken incidentally and not considered commercially important (Ref. 3798).
imageAttribution: "Haemulon boschmae (10.3897-zookeys.882.38449) Figure 5.jpg" by [Photographs CJE & AME] Robertson DR, Pérez-España H, Domínguez-Domínguez O, Estapé CJ, Estapé AM (2019) An update to the inventory of shore-fishes from the Parque Nacional Sistema Arrecifal Veracruzano, Veracruz, México. ZooKeys 882: 127-157. https://doi.org/10.3897/zookeys.882.38449 (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Adults generally occur in steep slopes and patch reefs. Diurnal species (Ref. 9626), they most commonly form moderate-sized feeding-schools over reef tops, rising above the bottom to feed on plankton, mainly copepods. Often seen with Chromis cyanea (Ref. 9710). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205). Rarely marketed (Ref. 3139).
imageAttribution: "Azurina multilineata 345286514.jpg" by Dan Schofield (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Found on sandy bottom, living in colonies (Ref. 26938). Feed on plankton and detritus (Ref. 26938). They retreat into burrow when frightened (Ref. 9710). Probably spawn during warm season (Ref. 6521). Larval life spans 6-8 months (Ref. 6521).
imageAttribution: "Heteroconger longissimus 117282720.jpg" by Julien Renoult (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Occurs in shallow waters (Ref. 103994 ). Nocturnal and lives in rocky bottoms (Ref. 103993), specifically in rocky reefs usually near reef crevices (Ref. 103994). May be solitary or in small groups. Inferred to have an omnivorous diet. Intestinal tract contained digested remains of crabs, filamentous algae, gastropod shells, and polychaete worms (Ref. 103994).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep

aboutText: Mostly found in seagrass beds in shallow, protected waters. Feeds primarily on epiphytes and seagrass blades, leaving crescent bite marks. Darts into seagrass when frightened (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Sparisoma radians 61925863.jpg" by Paul Prior (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits seagrass beds, coral rubble areas, and offshore reefs down to about 50 m. Feeds on a wide variety of small benthic invertebrates such as mollusks, crustaceans, worms and sessile tunicates, as well as some sea grasses (Ref. 3696). Highly esteemed food fish in the Caribbean (Ref. 3696).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Lactophrys trigonus - Naturhistorisches Museum, Braunschweig, Germany - DSC05011.JPG" by Daderot (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Inhabits offshore waters, usually from 18-127 m depth. Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Found frequently in coastal waters to 10 m depth, mainly in shallow estuaries. Capable of traveling long distances, occasionally leaps out of the water. Swims in midwater. Cruises slowly over the bottom, rooting out bivalves with its beak and wings when feeding (Ref. 7251). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 50449).
imageAttribution: "Myliobatis freminvillei (FDA 121).jpg" by The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in clear rocky or coral reefs (Ref. 5217) and mangroves (Ref. 26340). Often forming schools (Ref. 3798). Nocturnal feeder on crabs, gastropods, starfish and annelid worms (Ref. 5521). Marketed fresh (Ref. 3798).
imageAttribution: "Haemulon carbonarium - pone.0010676.g091.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Common in mangrove-lined lagoons; also found over shallow mud and sand grounds in marine areas. Juveniles common in hypersaline lagoons and in brackish water (Ref. 5217). Probably feeds on small benthic invertebrates. Marketed fresh but not highly esteemed. Minimum depth from Ref. 26912.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution:
"Manuscript of Henry Weed Fowler on the fishes of the Philippines, unpublished, circa 1930-1941 (IA manuscripthenry00fowldp).pdf" by
Fowler, Henry Weed, 1878-1965
(Public domain).

aboutText: Found in the recesses of coral reefs. Secretive (Ref. 9710).
Remains in deep recesses during the day. Rarely seen (Ref. 9710). Found in holes and caves in coral reefs (Ref. 26938).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Plectrypops retrospinis - pone.0010676.g037.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Inhabits weedy or sandy bottoms of shallow inshore waters (Ref. 9710). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Syngnathus dawsoni in La Parguera, Lajas, Puerto Rico.jpg" by National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration CCMA Biogeography Team (Public domain).

aboutText: Found on continental and insular shelves, often near drop-offs on the outer edges of reefs. May rest on the bottom (Ref. 9710). Usually found in reefs, at less than 30 m deep (Ref 26938). Feed on bony fishes, including bigeyes (Priacanthidae). Viviparous (Ref. 50449). Size at birth below 73 cm. A dangerous species implicated in an abortive attack on divers in the Caribbean. Meat is prepared dried salted for human consumption, hides for leather, liver for oil, carcasses for fish meal.
imageAttribution: "Carcharhinus perezii lower teeth.jpg" by D Ross Robertson (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits reefs and marginal habitats such as seagrass beds (Ref. 43205). Diet consists of seagrass, sponges, crabs and other crustaceans, mollusks, polychaete worms, sea urchins, starfishes, hydroids and algae (Ref. 13442). Monogamous (Ref. 52884).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Canthigaster rostrata 347142497.jpg" by Pauline Walsh Jacobson (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits bays and shallow coastal waters.
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Symphurus arawak - pone.0010676.g189.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Adults are found over soft bottoms of the continental shelf, often inshore and in estuaries. Also pelagic and encountered throughout the water column (Ref. 9626). May feed on plankton by ram-filtering. Juveniles feed mainly on planktonic crustaceans and chaetognaths, to a minor extent on benthic crustaceans and polychaetes, besides scales taken from larger fishes (Ref. 30206, 40402). Mature females generally spawn in summer. Eggs are pelagic (Ref. 35237). Marketed mostly fresh.
imageAttribution: "Oligoplites saliens.jpg" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: Collected from a small patch of reef surrounded by sand (Ref. 40885).
A solitary species (Ref. 26340) inhabiting rocky and reef areas.
imageAttribution: "Zookeys-409-071-g001 (14310864892).jpg" by Phylogeny Figures (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Adults are found over sandy and muddy bottoms in coastal waters and river mouths (Ref. 35237). Juveniles often found in estuaries (9626). Spawning generally occurs in summer. Eggs are pelagic (Ref. 35237). Good food fish, consumed fresh (Ref. 9626).

Most abundant in clear waters around coral reefs, occasionally forming schools. Feeds on clupeoids (Harengula, Jenkinsia and Opisthonema) and atherinids (Allanetta [= Craterocephalus), squids and shrimps. Good food and game fish (Ref. 5521). Utilized fresh, smoked and frozen; consumed pan-fried, broiled and baked (Ref. 9987). Sometimes called the "Painted Mackerel" (Ref. 13442).
imageAttribution: "FMIB 37836 Scomberomorus Regalis (Bloch) Pintado, Spotted Cero, Sierra.jpeg" by Bert Fesler (Public domain).

aboutText: A benthic and solitary species (Ref. 26340) found commonly on reefs and rocky shore areas. Feeds on small fishes and crustaceans (Ref. 5521). At Fernando de Noronha Archipelago, off NE Brazil, forages for sally lightfoot crabs (Grapsus grapsus) on exposed reefs at ebb tide and in tide-pools mostly at daytime. Able to withstand up to 30 minutes out of water while foraging, uses four main tactics both in and out of the water. Searches for prey at pool rims and rock bases poking into crevices and holes, stealthily approaches previously sighted prey, chases prey and ambushes prey from under rocks and crevices. Hunting success varies with employed tactic, but overall success is about 50%. May move up to 6 meters in about 1 hour while foraging on the exposed reef. Its crab hunting is mostly visually guided and a fish darting nearby a stealthily foraging moray may cause it to miss the strike; the missed crab may be chased up to 5 m on the reef. Able to strike with its body partly or entirely out of the water, usually strikes from a distance of 5 to10 centimeters. Small crabs are swallowed whole, whereas larger ones are torn apart by a combination of tugging, rotating, knotting, and thrashing movements. Handling time is related to prey size, the largest crabs (carapace width 2.3-3.2 times larger than moray’s head width) broken up and swallowed within 90 to 240 seconds. Attracted to plastic or rubber decoys dragged on a nylon string nearby, striking at these (Ref. 50922).
imageAttribution:
"Chain Moray Eel Echidna Catenata (223581583).jpeg" by
Kris Mikael Krister
(CC BY 3.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0

aboutText: Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205). Feed mostly on amphipods and other small crustaceans (Ref. 27549).
imageAttribution: "Syngnathus louisianae (S0591) (12658764723).jpg" by Smithsonian Environmental Research Center (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits rocky and coral reefs, often inside tube sponges (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Starksia ocellata range.png" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Commonly found in bays, tidal creeks and protected coastal waters, especially on seagrass beds, and in brackish water. Rare or absent on coral reefs. Does not form schools, but may form huge aggregates. Hides in the sand when frightened (Ref. 9710). Feeds mainly on bivalves, gastropods, foraminiferans and several other benthic invertebrates specially crustaceans, which it crushes with its powerful teeth (Ref. 35237). To ward off predators, it inflates itself like a balloon. Highly toxic; used to poison cats and dogs (Ref. 2861).
imageAttribution: "Sphoeroides testudineus (checkered pufferfish) (San Salvador Island, Bahamas).jpg" by James St. John (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Found over sandy and rubble bottoms; occasionally on coral reefs and rocky areas (Ref. 3798). Rarely in clear oceanic insular areas (Ref. 5217). Often found in schools (Ref. 3798). Juveniles encountered near the shore, over sandy bottoms near seagrass beds (Ref. 9626). Feeds on benthic invertebrates (Ref. 3798). Marketed fresh (Ref. 3798).
imageAttribution: "Haemulon steindachneri.jpg" by rmcminds (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Normally occurs in rubble areas. Feeds on various types of algae. Oviparous (Ref. 205), monogamous (Ref. 52884). Retreats into holes when frightened (Ref. 9710). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35408).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Cherub fish Centropyge argi.jpg" by Brian Gratwicke (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Found in crevices and on rubble to depths of at least 40 m (Ref. 13121).
imageAttribution: "Starksia sluiteri range.png" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Prefers rocky bottoms (Ref. 26340). Also found in and around coral reefs.
imageAttribution:
"Enchelycore carychroa, Head (Caribbean Chestnut Moray).jpg" by
Belize Larval-Fish Group, Smithsonian Institution
(CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Found on shallow, soft bottoms. Ovoviviparous (Ref. 50449). Flesh considered low quality (Ref. 5217). Traded as an aquarium fish at Ceará, Brazil (Ref. 49392).
imageAttribution: "Pseudobatos percellens A742548.jpg" by Rjcastillo (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits muddy bottoms (mixed mud, shell and leaf litter) (Ref. 93147).

Abundant on reef tops and in shallow rocky areas. Found to depths of at least 25 m (Ref. 26938). Also found in Sargassum beds (Ref. 9626). Solitary and wary and can be difficult to approach (Ref. 26938). The tricolored pattern of the initial phase is similar to that of the juveniles of the yellowmouth grouper, Mycteroperca interstitialis, an aggressive mimic (Ref. 43465). A protogynous hermaphrodite (Ref. 55367). Generally of no interest to fisheries because of its small average size (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Halichoeres maculipinna 304496434.jpg" by Pauline Walsh Jacobson (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Adults inhabit offshore reefs on relatively level bottoms, often covered with coralline algae rhodoliths (Ref. 7247). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205).
Chiefly marine, common around river mouths and also entering river estuaries. Feeds on small fishes and invertebrates, such as crustaceans. Marketed fresh.
imageAttribution: "Bagre bagre.png" by Clara Costa D'Elia (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs, tide pools, limestone caves, algae-covered rocks and isolated patch reefs surrounded by white sand (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Coryphopterus dicrus - pone.0010676.g164.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Juveniles inhabit turtle grass beds, mangrove areas, and shallow waters among soft corals and coral reefs. Adults are found on rocky bottoms with high relief. Probably feeds on plankton (no information is available on the food of this species). The max weight of 4 kg given in Ref. 5222 appears too low. The species is a popular game fish in southern Brazil for both spear fishermen and rod-and-reel anglers. Fish of 4-7 kg are common, with the spear fishing record at 10.1 kg (Capt. Eduardo Baumeier, pers. comm., 2001).
imageAttribution: "Mycteroperca acutirostris 258750851.jpg" by James-LaFontaine (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Adults are found in open waters but also near the coast (Ref. 9293, 11230). Form schools. Feed on almost all forms of fish and zooplankton; also takes crustaceans and squid (Ref. 2850). Sexual maturity is reached in 4-5 months (3 for captive fish) (Ref. 11441). Spawn in the open sea and probably approximate to the coast when water temperature rises (Ref. 9293). Eggs and larvae are pelagic (Ref. 6755). Attracting devices such as floating bundles of bamboo reeds or cork planks are used to concentrate dolphin fish before the nets are set. Marketed frozen (Ref. 9987) and fresh and is of high value (Ref. 9293). Caught by trolling and on tuna longlines; also occasionally with drift nets (Ref. 9846).
imageAttribution: "Coryphaena hippurus meat.JPG" by ZeWrestler (Public domain).

aboutText: Widely distributed species occurs on seaward reefs.
imageAttribution:
"Bulletin - United States National Museum (IA bulletinunit20211953unit).pdf" by
United States National Museum
Smithsonian Institution
United States. Dept. of the Interior
(Public domain).

aboutText: An inshore, surface-dwelling species forming sizeable schools, frequently entering estuaries (Ref. 3723). Uncommon over reefs (Ref. 9710). Omnivorous, feeds on algae and other small animals (Ref. 3723). Caught with 'mandinga' nets in Venezuela, often using lights (Ref. 3723). Edible but not really appreciated (Ref. 3723).
imageAttribution: "Hyporhamphus unifasciatus.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Prefers clear insular waters of oceanic islands (Ref. 9626). Has a commensal relationship with the conch shell Lobatus gigas (where it is found in the mantle cavity) and with the bivalve Atrina rigida (Ref. 9626). Emerges at night from the conch shell to feed on small crustaceans. Males brood the eggs in their mouths. (Ref. 26938).
imageAttribution: "Astrapogon stellatus" by Dr. William H. Longley is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Numerous in the estuarine areas of the Brazilian coast; stays motionless in the sandy or muddy bottoms; the venom is injected in the flesh of the victim when pressure is applied on the gland (Ref. 55461).
imageAttribution:
"Archivos do Museu Nacional do Rio de Janeiro (IA archivosdomuseu21muse).pdf" by
Museu Nacional (Brazil)
(Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits small patch reefs (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "FMIB 38076 Scorpaena albifimbria Evermann & Marsh.jpeg" by Barton Warren Evermann / Millard Caleb Marsh (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in coastal waters, over sand, rock or mud bottoms (Ref. 13608). Juveniles found near the shore, associated with Thalassia beds (Ref. 9626). Also found in brackish water (Ref. 3798). Feeds on crustaceans, mollusks, polychaete worms, fishes, and other invertebrates (Ref. 9626). Well appreciated as a food fish; marketed fresh and salted (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution:
"Archivos do Museu Nacional do Rio de Janeiro (IA archivosdomuseu21muse).pdf" by
Museu Nacional (Brazil)
(Public domain).

aboutText: Found on sand and rubble on a sloping area (Ref. 103699).
imageAttribution: "Coryphopterus curasub.jpg" by Ian Silver-Gorges (A) and D. R. Robertson and C. C. Baldwin (B–D). (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits clear inshore and offshore reefs (Ref. 9710) and adjacent grass beds, often in schools (Ref. 3798). Feeds on crustaceans (Ref. 3798). Marketed fresh (Ref. 3798).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Haemulon melanurum - Sanc1581.jpg" by NOAA Photo Library (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: A benthopelagic species found on continental and insular shelves; enters bays and estuaries; forms huge schools inshore (Ref. 114953). Feeds mainly on benthic invertebrates and molluscs (implicated in damaging seagrass beds) (Ref. 93252, 114953). Jumps occasionally, landing with a loud smack, probably as a territorial display. Migrates south in large schools that disappear off northern Florida, USA and are not reported from Caribbean Is.; tagged fish have been recovered in northern South America (Ref. 7251). Population in the Gulf of Mexico migrates clockwise; schools of up to 10,000 rays leave west coast of Florida for Yucatan, Mexico in the fall (Ref. 7251). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 50449).
imageAttribution: "Rhinoptera bonasus, cownose stingray teeth & mouthparts.jpg" by James St. John (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits seaward reef slopes; occasionally on shallow patch reefs (Ref. 9710). Found in large midwater aggregations feeding on plankton (Ref. 9710), small jellyfishes, pteropods, pelagic tunicates and various invertebrate larvae (Ref. 13442). Form leks during breeding (Ref. 55367). Also caught with beach nets (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Clepticus parrae, St. Croix.jpg" by NOAA (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs and hard bottom areas. Observed in feeding aggregations well above the reef. Feeds mainly in midwater on zooplankton (copepods, pelagic tunicates, shrimps and shrimp larvae). Retreats instantly when alarmed (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Paranthias furcifer (FDA 179).jpg" by The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in coastal waters, forming dense schools along beaches in water of 1 m or less.
imageAttribution:
"Il Friuli giornale politico-amministrativo-letterario-commerciale n. 63 (1896) (IA IlFriuli-63 1896).pdf" by
Il Friuli
(Public domain).

aboutText: Found over soft bottoms (Ref. 9626) of coastal waters. Secretive but approachable (Ref. 9710). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35408).
imageAttribution: "Cubbyu ( Pareques umbrosus ).jpg" by NOAA's Fisheries Collection / NOAA Photo Library (Public domain).

aboutText: Adults are found mainly around ledges over rocky bottoms or around reefs. Young sometimes inhabit mangrove areas. Maximum depth from Ref. 126840. Feed mainly on fishes, shrimps and crabs (Ref. 55) and lobsters using their strong canines (Ref. 123493). A wary fish, not easily approached underwater (Ref. 13442).
imageAttribution: "Lutjanus cyanopterus.jpg" by Henrya (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

"Ogilbia sabaji - pone.0010676.g027.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: An inshore shark found over rocky bottoms, entering estuaries and river mouths. Feeds on small schooling fishes including herring, anchovies and croakers. Viviparous, generally 4 embryos per brood; size at birth 38 to 41 cm. 244 cm record unconfirmed.
imageAttribution: "Isogomphodon oxyrhynchus (Müller & Henle, 1839) 2432753577.jpg" by Naturalis Biodiversity Center (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en
"Starksia sella range.png" by Unknown authorUnknown author (Public domain).

aboutText: Sand-dweller (Ref. 5521).
Inhabits open bottoms composed of sand and fine silt (Ref. 5521). Observed to share a burrow with shrimps.
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs mostly on deep coral reefs and steep, coral-covered slopes.
imageAttribution: "Scorpaenodes tredecimspinosus.jpg" by Ellen Muller (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Known from offshore , deep waters, with adults trawled between 33-110 m (Ref. 37108). Intertidal observations were also reported (Ref. 49162).
imageAttribution: "Biodiversity Assessment of the Fishes of Saba Bank Atoll, Netherlands Antilles.pdf" by Jeffrey T. Williams; Kent E. Carpenter; James L. Van Tassell; Paul Hoetjes; Wes Toller; Peter Etnoyer; Michael Smith, edited by Brian Gratwicke (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Adults are found in coastal waters in muddy habitats (Ref. 48635). Minimum depth of 0m (Ref. 81209). Fin spines highly venomous, may cause human death (Ref. 30573).
imageAttribution: "Pterois miles 484773446" by Dimitri Brosens is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en/.

aboutText: Inhabit patch reefs or other deeper water coral formations. Usually associated with Condylactis gigantea (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Malacoctenus boehlkei - pone.0010676.g147.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Inhabits shallow reefs or rocky areas. Found in loose aggregations (Ref. 9710). Mainly diurnal. Ingests sand when feeding on algae (Ref. 13442). The spine on both sides of the caudal peduncle may inflict painful wounds (Ref. 5217). Minimum depth reported from Ref. 27115. Larvae are planktonic (Ref. 47377). At Fernando de Noronha Archipelago in southwestern Atlantic, juveniles hold cleaning stations together with the blue tang (Acanthurus coeruleus) and sergeant major (Abudefduf saxatilis) and graze algae as well as pick molted skin and parasites from green turtles (Chelonia mydas). This behavior is preceded by a characteristic inspection usually followed by feeding nips on the turtles’ skin (head, limbs, and tail), as well as on the carapace. The most inspected and cleaned body parts are the flippers (Ref. 51385).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Acanthurus chirurgus - 1700-1880 - Print - Iconographia Zoologica - Special Collections University of Amsterdam - UBA01 IZ13700033.tif" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs in coastal areas, but also in lagoons and off river mouths, perhaps entering water of lowered salinity (Ref. 12225). In Southwestern Atlantic preys on proportionally large clupeoid fishes and caridean shrimps. Fishes are distinctively folded in the stomachs of this herring, in about 90% of the cases with their heads and tails pointing head-wards the predator. This herring stands out as the only small-sized species among the predaceous fish-eating clupeiforms. The well developed canine-like teeth are likely related to a piscivorous diet and may play a role in the folding and orientation of fish during prey handling and swallowing (Ref. 51383). Spawns during the northern winter (Ref. 37032).
imageAttribution: "Large-bodied sabre-toothed anchovies reveal unanticipated ecological diversity in early Palaeogene teleosts.pdf" by Capobianco, Alessio; Beckett, Hermione T.; Steurbaut, Etienne; Gingerich, Philip D.; Carnevale, Giorgio; Friedman, Matt (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0
"Lipogramma anabantoides.jpg" by Carole C. Baldwin, Luke Tornabene, D. Ross Robertson, Ai Nonaka, Grant Gilmore (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits sandy bottoms with rocks or boulders (Ref. 5521), seagrass beds, and patch reefs (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Malacoctenus gilli.jpeg" by Barton Warren Evermann / Millard Caleb Marsh (Public domain).

aboutText: Found on coral, gravel, or shell bottoms (Ref. 9626). Found near open basket stars at night (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Phaeoptyx pigmentaria" by William Beebe is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Adults inhabit rocky shores exposed to wave action. Often in tide pools. Feed primarily on algae and detritus. Territorial and pugnacious. Generally common (Ref. 9710). Oviparous, distinct pairing during breeding (Ref. 205). Eggs are demersal and adhere to the substrate (Ref. 205). Males guard and aerate the eggs (Ref. 205). Taken incidentally in traps and small-meshed beach nets (Ref. 5217).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Stegastes adustus 176364656.jpg" by Dan Schofield (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Inhabits rock and sand bottoms or the eroding edges of weed beds. Egg mass incubated in mouth of males (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Opistognathus whitehursti - pone.0010676.g067.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Abundant in seagrass beds along coasts. Northern populations move offshore during colder months (Ref. 26938). Found mostly at 5 m or less (Ref. 126547). Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205). Feeds mostly on small grass shrimp and other small crustaceans (Ref. 93252).
imageAttribution: "Syngnathus floridae (S0241) (12658632285).jpg" by Smithsonian Environmental Research Center (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Found on soft bottoms of shelf waters (Ref. 5217). Largest specimen examined was 93.3 cm TL, a 100 cm TL specimen was reported (Ref. 54133).
imageAttribution: "De novis speciebus piscium (Tab. XIII) (7972094908).jpg" by Accademia delle scienze dell'Istituto di Bologna; Ranzani, Camillo. (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits inshore reefs and tide pools. Hides in small recesses. Common, but rarely seen (Ref. 9710). Nocturnal species (Ref. 26938). Intertidal observations were also reported (Ref. 49162).
imageAttribution: "Neoniphon vexillarium" by Jean-Paul Boerekamps is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: A shallow water species (1-51 m) (Ref. 26268), found over mud or sand bottoms (Ref. 5217) where it feeds on small benthic invertebrates likes annelids and crustaceans. Normally found along sand banks near the mouth of rivers (Ref. 35237).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution:
"Manuscript of Henry Weed Fowler on the fishes of the Philippines, unpublished, circa 1930-1941 (IA manuscripthenry00fowlcy).pdf" by
Fowler, Henry Weed, 1878-1965
(Public domain).

aboutText: Occurs mainly near patches of reef and rock. Relatively uncommon (Ref. 9710). Smallest frogfish in the western Atlantic (Ref. 26938).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Antennarius pauciradiatus - pone.0010676.g029.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Found over mud and sand bottoms (Ref. 3792). Juveniles up to 8 cm are pelagic (Ref. 9626). Feeds mainly on benthic invertebrates (Ref. 3792). Flesh considered good quality; marketed fresh and frozen (Ref. 3792). In southwestern Brazil found in 112 m depth (Ref. 47377).
imageAttribution: "Upeneus parvus - Flickr - NOAA Photo Library.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC. (Public domain).

aboutText: Inhabits coral reefs or isolated coral head habitats (Ref. 5521).
imageAttribution: "Lythrypnus elasson - pone.0010676.g173.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Inhabit coastal waters (Ref. 5217). Adults are found in shallow depth near sand; juveniles often in rockpools (Ref. 86997). Oviparous, eggs are pelagic and non-adhesive (Ref. 205).
imageAttribution: "Treubia (IA CFGEDI1921010100219210001).pdf" by Unknown (Public domain).

aboutText: A schooling species usually occurring inshore. Feeds on zooplankton. Preyed upon by many larger fishes and squids (Ref. 5521). Important as live bait for scombrids (Ref. 26340). In 1937, at Kingston, Jamaica it spawns during the winter months. Travels long distance (Ref. 37032).
imageAttribution: "Jenkinsia lamprotaenia.jpg" by Donald Davesne (CC BY 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0

aboutText: Found on soft bottom (Ref. 5217). Synchronously hermaphroditic. Good food fish (Ref. 5217).
imageAttribution: "Diplectrum bivittatum.jpg" by SEFSC Pascagoula Laboratory; Collection of Brandi Noble, NOAA/NMFS/SEFSC (Public domain).

aboutText: Shallow grass flats, especially in association with Zostera and other seagrass; periodically found in floating vegetation. Diurnal, males more site faithful than females. Ovoviviparous (Ref. 205). The male carries the eggs in a brood pouch which is found under the tail (Ref. 205). Specimens lose their skin filaments in aquaria (Ref. 30915). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35420). Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128812.
imageAttribution: "Hippocampus zosterae.jpg" by Will Thomas (CC BY 2.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0

aboutText: Inhabits shallow beds of turtle grass (Thalassia). Common but seldom noticed (Ref. 9710).
imageAttribution: "Doratonotus megalepis - pone.0010676.g112.png" by Williams, J. T.; Carpenter, K. E.; Van Tassell, J. L.; Hoetjes, P.; Toller, W.; Etnoyer, P.; Smith, M. (CC BY 2.5). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.5

aboutText: Lives in rubble areas covered by mats of algae and sponges or in beds of finger coral. Generally found in less than 10 m depth (Ref. 13628).
imageAttribution: "Environmental appendix- Miami-Dade County Back Bay coastal storm risk management feasibility study, Miami-Dade County, Florida- Appendix D - USACE-p16021coll7-14462.pdf" by United States. Army. Corps of Engineers. Norfolk District (Public domain).

aboutText: Maximum depth from Ref. 52834. Inhabits seagrass beds and open bottom, mostly in shallow water. Larger adults live in deeper water. Not commonly marketed for food (Ref. 52834).
imageAttribution: "Nicholsina usta" by Dr. William H. Longley is marked with CC0 1.0. To view the terms, visit https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/.

aboutText: Juveniles are encountered under ledges, or in holes of outer lagoon patch reefs or semi-protected areas of exposed channels and outer reef flats. Subadults move to reef front holes and surge channels. Large adults inhabit ledges and caves in areas of rich coral growth on clear lagoon, channel, or seaward reefs (Ref. 6113). Benthopelagic (Ref. 58302). Feed on sponges and other encrusting organisms (Ref. 6113); also on tunicates. Form pairs. Young and adults may clean much larger fishes such as sunfish (Ref. 48636). Frequently exported through the aquarium trade. Juveniles are distinguished by a white dorsal-fin margin (Ref. 48391).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
imageAttribution: "Pomacanthus imperator 419892749.jpg" by Jean-Paul Boerekamps (CC0). http://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/deed.en

aboutText: Found in deep recesses of caves in the outer reef slope (Ref. 1602). Benthopelagic (Ref. 58302). Nocturnal species (Ref. 7300). Swims upside-down on ceilings (Ref. 48635). Solitary or in pairs (Ref 90102).
imageAttribution: "FMIB 42450 Amia evermanni (Jordan & Snyder); from the type.jpeg" by David Starr Jordan / Barton Warren Evermann (Public domain).

aboutText: Obligate sponge dwellers. Live deep within canals of the sponges, near the openings to the incurrent canals. Sponges provide species with protection from predators and an available food source in many invertebrates. Eggs found within sponge or small plankton brought through incurrent pores (Ref. 92840).

Inhabits sandy areas with coral rubble or seagrasses, usually near patch reefs (Ref. 9710). Shallow coastal waters to depths of 110 m (Ref. 26938). Lies motionless on the bottom, moving only when frightened (Ref. 9710). Larvae are attracted to lights at night but are difficult to see because they are transparent (Ref. 26938). About one-third of the diet consists of fishes; the rest of its food is crustaceans: crabs, shrimps, amphipods, and mantis shrimps (Ref. 13442).
distinctiveFeatures: Body shape: short and / or deep
